elements.sent-2411-001: The shared topology of human skeletons motivated the recent investigation of graph convolutional network (GCN) solutions for action recognition.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the existing GCNs rely on the binary connection of two neighbouring vertices (joints) formed by an edge (bone), overlooking the potential of constructing multi-vertex convolution structures.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper we address this oversight and explore the merits of a hyper-graph convolutional network (Hyper-GCN) to achieve the aggregation of rich semantic information conveyed by skeleton vertices.
elements.sent-2411-004: In particular, our Hyper-GCN adaptively optimises multi-scale hyper-graphs during training, revealing the action-driven multi-vertex relations.
elements.sent-2411-005: Besides, virtual connections are often designed to support efficient feature aggregation, implicitly extending the spectrum of dependencies within the skeleton.
elements.sent-2411-006: By injecting virtual connections into hyper-graphs, the semantic clues of diverse action categories can be highlighted.
elements.sent-2411-007: The results of experiments conducted on the NTU-60, NTU-120, and NW-UCLA datasets, demonstrate the merits of our Hyper-GCN, compared to the state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-008: Specifically, we outperform the existing solutions on NTU-120, achieving 90.2\% and 91.4\% in terms of the top-1 recognition accuracy on X-Sub and X-Set.
elements.sent-2411-009: Skeleton-based human action recognition is a popular research topic in artificial intelligence, with practical applications in video understanding, video surveillance, human-computer interaction, robot vision, VR, and AR~\cite{human_computer_interaction_1, human_computer_interaction_2, VR, skeletalDetection, skeletonViolenceDetection, skeletonVideoSurveillance, skeletonVideoSurvillance2 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: In general, a skeleton sequence contains a series of 2D or 3D coordinates, which can easily be collected by low-cost depth sensors or obtained by video-based pose estimation algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-011: Compared to RGB and optical flow images, skeleton data, which represents the basic physical structure of a human being, is of lower dimension and higher efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-012: Moreover, it is robust to illumination changes and scene variations.
elements.sent-2411-013: For these reasons, the adoption of this structural data is very popular in skeleton-based action recognition~\cite{HRNN, DRNN, ST-LSTM, NTU60, InfoGCN, CTR-GCN, DS-GCN .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, trim=0mm 25mm 0mm 0mm]{figures/motivation.pdf} \caption{Illustration of our Hyper-GCN. In (a), dotted lines represent the virtual connections. Each coloured part containing multiple joints represents a hyper-graph with hyper-edges. (b) provides an intuitive comparison between normal graph convolution and hyper-graph convolution operations with the same connection degree.} \label{motivation}
elements.sent-2411-014: To facilitate skeleton-based action recognition, both Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)~\cite{HRNN, DRNN, ST-LSTM, NTU60 and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)~\cite{P_CNN, 2s-CNN, 3dCNN have been well explored.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, RNNs themselves cannot depict the intrinsic skeleton topology.
elements.sent-2411-016: The learned filters of CNNs, on the other hand, neglect the spatio-temporal structure of the skeleton.
elements.sent-2411-017: Drawing on these observations, recent studies have focused on how to model the skeleton topology directly.
elements.sent-2411-018: In principle, the physical topology of human joints and bones can be consistently represented by a graph.
elements.sent-2411-019: Accordingly, the graph convolutional network (GCN) is typically introduced to achieve the aggregation of feature information conveyed by skeleton joints~\cite{ST-GCN, CTR-GCN, HD-GCN .}
elements.sent-2411-020: In general, two neighbouring joints can pass messages through their shared bone.
elements.sent-2411-021: In graph terms, this corresponds to the exchange of information between two vertices along their connecting edge.
elements.sent-2411-022: Besides the physical skeleton topology, attempts have been made to explore implicit relationships among joints~\cite{2s-AGCN, CTR-GCN, InfoGCN, HD-GCN , suggesting different variants of skeleton topology.}
elements.sent-2411-023: In principle, these approaches assume a binary connection between each connected vertex pair.
elements.sent-2411-024: Mathematically, the constructed topology in the adjacency matrix is represented by a normal graph.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, human actions are jointly defined by several joints.
elements.sent-2411-026: Hence, human actions encompass not only binary relations between vertex pairs but also multi-joint relationships.
elements.sent-2411-027: For instance, the action primitive starting running is manifest in the human raising the left hand while the right leg steps forward, as shown in Figure~\ref{motivation (a).}
elements.sent-2411-028: The binary connections are not sufficient to capture the synergistic interaction of multiple joints.
elements.sent-2411-029: This strongly argues for constructing feature aggregation paths involving multiple vertices.
elements.sent-2411-030: Accordingly, we propose to construct a hyper-graph to depict the skeleton topology and take advantage of the outstanding performance of hyper-graph analysis techniques ~\cite{dynamicHypergraph, hypergraphAttention, multilevelHypergraph, HGNN+ .}
elements.sent-2411-031: The hyper-graph topology involves multiple node connections, rather than binary connections of a normal graph.
elements.sent-2411-032: As shown in Figure~\ref{motivation (a), in a hyper-graph, a hyper-edge can link more than two vertices, which has the capacity to represent complex collaborative relations among human joints.}
elements.sent-2411-033: As one hyper-edge associates multiple vertices, a single hyper-graph convolution enables aggregating all the features along the hyper-edges.
elements.sent-2411-034: An illustration is provided in Figure~\ref{motivation (b), where the normal graph and hyper-graph are presented to demonstrate their differences in passing the information conveyed by the vertex features.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Conceptually, for a fair comparison, we typically set each hyper-edge to contain only 2 vertices, which is the same as the normal graph.
elements.sent-2411-036: In the normal graph convolution, after 2-layer aggregation, the information of the green vertex is spread to 2 other vertices.
elements.sent-2411-037: In contrast, in a hyper-graph convolution, the information of the green vertex spreads to 4 vertices including itself, creating an extended receptive field.
elements.sent-2411-038: Theoretically, by applying a hyper-graph for modelling joint aggregation on a skeleton, improved efficiency can be obtained during learning the action semantics.
elements.sent-2411-039: Besides its information aggregation mode, the capacity of an action recognition system is also limited by its input features.
elements.sent-2411-040: In general, the number of skeleton joints is fixed in existing benchmarks, e.g., 25 for NTU-120~\cite{NTU120 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: The entire recognition process relies on the interactions among the involved joints.
elements.sent-2411-042: In the domain of artistic puppetry, the history of driving actions, such as Shadow Play~\footnote{\url{https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shadow_play and Marionette~\footnote{\url{https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marionette goes back more than 2000 years.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: This kind of art form provides an inspiration for involving additional 'hyper joints' which can drive or facilitate a better communication between existing joints.
elements.sent-2411-044: The underlying spirit is to alleviate the pressure of the real joints in storing and transferring semantics.
elements.sent-2411-045: Jointly with the hyper joints, real joints can focus more on storing neighbouring joint features and transferring the global information to the hyper joints.
elements.sent-2411-046: Interestingly, the class token in existing Transformers can also be considered as a hyper-token ~\cite{STS-Attention, 3Mformer, IIP-Transformer, MixFormer .}
elements.sent-2411-047: As shown in Figure~\ref{motivation , the skeleton is described as a marionette, where the actions are "controlled" by connecting hyper joints to the real joints.}
elements.sent-2411-048: This suggests that the hyper joints are not only able to capture the representation information of human action, but also reveal the implicit information between physically connected joints as hints for recognition.
elements.sent-2411-049: By endowing a hyper-graph with hyper joints, virtual connections are created to perform comprehensive hyper-graph convolutions.
elements.sent-2411-050: We construct Hyper-GCN based on the above design principles.
elements.sent-2411-051: Extensive experiments on 3 datasets, NTU RGB+D 60, NTU RGB+D 120, and NW-UCLA, are conducted for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-052: The results validate the merits of our proposed Hyper-GCN.
elements.sent-2411-053: The main contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-054: Compared with the normal graph, the efficiency of feature interaction is improved via multi-vertex aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-055: On all three public datasets used for experimentation, Hyper-GCN achieves the SOTA performance.

elements.sent-2411-072: Given a fixed representative capacity (number of channels), we believe it is necessary to involve additional virtual joints to balance the pressure of storing local and global information.
elements.sent-2411-073: In general, the input features in graph convolution are represented by $\mathbf{{F _{in }\in\mathbb{R ^{C\times T\times V}$, where $C$ represents the number of channels in the feature maps.}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Given the topology of a human skeleton, we usually define the graph $\mathcal{G =(\mathcal{V ,\mathcal{E )$, where $\mathcal{V}$ represents the set of joints and $\mathcal{E}$ represents the set of edges between joints.}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: For the set of edges $\mathcal{E}$, it is formulated as an adjacent matrix $A \in\mathbb{R ^{N\times N}$, where $N$ represents the number of human joints.}
elements.sent-2411-076: The normalised adjacency matrix is represented by $\hat{A \in\mathbb{R ^{N\times N}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The normalisation operation is formulated as follows: \begin{equation}\hat{A}=\Lambda^{-\frac{1}{2}}\ A \Lambda^{-\frac{1}{2}},\end{equation} where $\Lambda$ represents the diagonal matrix stored with the degree of every joint.
elements.sent-2411-078: The entire normal graph convolution process can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{F_{out}}=\sigma(\hat{A} \mathbf{F_{in}}W), \label{eq-2}\end{equation} where $W \in \mathbb{R ^{C \times C'}$ is the learnable parameters, representing the feature transformation patterns in the feature space.}

elements.sent-2411-081: Since a hyper-edge contains multiple joints, the corresponding topology can no longer be simply represented by an adjacency matrix.

elements.sent-2411-091: How to construct the hyper-graph is critical to better understanding human actions.
elements.sent-2411-092: As shown in Figure~\ref{HyperGC (b), we design an Adaptive Hyper-graph Construction Module (AHC-Module).}
elements.sent-2411-093: Given the feature $\mathbf{{F ^{' _{in }\in\mathbb{R ^{C\times T\times V}$, we first perform temporal pooling, and then the obtained spatial information is the evidence to determine the optimal hyper-graph construction.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: This can efficiently decouple the temporal and spatial clues.
elements.sent-2411-095: After performing temporal average-pooling, we can obtain $\mathbf{\bar{F ^{' _{in }\in\mathbb{R ^{C\times V}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: To adjust the scale of the input feature channels, we adopt a layer normalisation operation on the channel dimension.
elements.sent-2411-097: After that, we use two individual mapping functions to embed the features into the subspace for constructing the hyper-graph.
elements.sent-2411-098: This design enables preserving the original features to reflect their spatial relevance.
elements.sent-2411-099: The embedding operation can be formulated as follows: \begin{equation}P_{emb} = \Phi(LN(Pooling_T(\mathbf{F^{'}_{in}}))), W_{emb} = Tanh(\Psi(LN(Pooling_T(\mathbf{F^{'}_{in}})))),\end{equation} where $P_emb$ represents the relative position of the joints in the embedding space, and $W_emb$ represents the weights of hyper-edges.
elements.sent-2411-100: LN denotes the layer normalisation along the channel dimension.
elements.sent-2411-101: Tanh denotes the activation function to obtain the weight of each hyper-edge, limiting the values with the range of $[-1, 1]$.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth, trim=25mm 0mm 15mm 0mm]{figures/AHC-Module.pdf} \caption{Illustration of the hyper-graph construction process with the proposed AHC-Module. $N$ represents the number of joints.} \label{AHC-Module}
elements.sent-2411-102: In the embedding space, our AHC-Module utilises the Euclidean distance to measure the joint difference.
elements.sent-2411-103: For the mapping of hyper-edges and vertices, the reason we apply only one mapping function instead of two separate ones is to ensure that vertices and hyper-edges are within the same semantic space.
elements.sent-2411-104: Define the set of joints $P_emb = \{v_0 \in \mathbb{R ^{C_h , v_1 \in \mathbb{R ^{C_h , ...}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: In principle, we need to transform the distance matrix into a probability hyper-grap0h, which determines whether the specific joints are contained with the same hyper-edges.
elements.sent-2411-106: In order to guarantee that the model is trainable, we use the softmax operation to assign the probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-107: However, directly using the softmax would inevitably result in each joint belonging to all hyper-edges to some extent.
elements.sent-2411-108: Since this outcome is undesirable, we introduce constraints on each joint to limit the number of hyper-edges it can belong to, preventing excessive connections.
elements.sent-2411-109: We retain only the $K$ nearest hyper-edges represented by each joint in the semantic space for probability estimation.
elements.sent-2411-110: Given the row vector $\mathbf{m _i$ in the distance matrix $M$, which represents the distances between joint $i$ and the other joints, we select the indices of the minimum $K$ joints from $\mathbf{m _i$ and add them to the set $set^K_{i}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Based on $set^K_{i}$, $h_{i,j}$ in the incidence matrix $H \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times N}$ of hyper-graph, can be calculated as follows: \begin{equation}h_{i,j} =  \frac{e^{-m_{i,j}}}{\sum_{k \in set^K_{i}}{e^{-m_{i,k}}}}, j \in set^K_{i}, 0, j \notin set^K_{i} \end{equation} The shape of our incidence matrix is consistent with the adjacency matrix of the normal graph.}
elements.sent-2411-112: While our topology with hyper-graph contains more high-order information.
elements.sent-2411-113: The entire process is illustrated in the Figure~\ref{AHC-Module .}
elements.sent-2411-114: As our AHC-Module can depict the topology into a hyper-graph at different scales by adjusting the value of $K$.
elements.sent-2411-115: To accommodate the semantic information reflected by these varying scales, we further propose the Multi-Scale Hyper-graph Convolution (MS-HGC), as shown in Figure~\ref{HyperGC (a).}
elements.sent-2411-116: We use different branches to independently perform hyper-graph convolution on the topological information represented by the multi-scale hyper-graphs, thereby enhancing the computational efficiency of the Hyper-GCN.
elements.sent-2411-117: Besides the hyper-graph, we incorporate the physical topology to emphasise the natural physical relations of a human being's skeleton.
elements.sent-2411-118: To achieve this objective, existing methods~\cite{ST-GCN, 2s-AGCN, CTR-GCN, HD-GCN divide the physical topology of the human body into 3 subsets.}
elements.sent-2411-119: They are represented by $S=\{s_{id , s_{cf , s_{cp}\}$, where $s_{id}$, $s_{cf}$ and $s_{cp}$ denote the identity, centrifugal, and centripetal joint subsets.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: In our MS-HGC, to ensure that the integrated physical topology in each branch remains complete, we merge them into a single set.
elements.sent-2411-121: We divide the input feature into 8 separate branches along the channel dimension, which are processed by 8 MS-HGC parallelly, delivering 8 multi-scale hyper-graphs.
elements.sent-2411-122: The operation of MS-HGC can be formulated as follows: \begin{equation}\mathbf{F_{out}} = \uplus^8_{i=1} {(\hat{A_i} + \alpha_i \cdot \hat{H_i}) \mathbf{F^{[(i-1) \times C^{'} + 1, ..., i \times C^{'}]}_{in}} W_i},\end{equation} where $\mathbf{[(i-1)\times C^{' +1, ..., i\times C^{' ]}$ represents the channels of $\mathbf{F_{in }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-123: It is worth emphasising that incorporating learnable joints among different samples is essential for enhancing the model capacity, as these learnable joints capture generalised features of human actions.
elements.sent-2411-124: This not only enriches the semantic information but also facilitates easier interaction connections among real joints.
elements.sent-2411-125: Therefore, we introduce the hyper-joints which are to participate in the hyper-graph convolution as shown in Figure~\ref{HyperGC (a).}
elements.sent-2411-126: The shape of hyper-joints is consistent with the feature $\mathbf{F_{p \in \mathbb{R ^{C^{' \times T\times V}$, which is described as $\mathbf{F_{h \in \mathbb{R ^{C^{' \times T\times V_h}$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-127: Specifically, the learning of hyper-joints is supervised by the loss function, which guides these hyper-joints to support generalisable driven features embedded within large amounts of data.
elements.sent-2411-128: Furthermore, we set independent hyper-joints at each layer of Hyper-GCN, aiming to harmonise features at different depths.
elements.sent-2411-129: Typically, these hyper-joints are involved in spatial hyper-graph convolution rather than in temporal convolution.
elements.sent-2411-130: To diversify these hyper-joints, we propose the Divergence Loss for hyper-joints optimisation to mitigate their homogenisation.
elements.sent-2411-131: We adopt a cosine matrix $C \in \mathbb{R ^{V_h \times V_h}$ to measure the differences between hyper-joints, which can be formulated as follows: \begin{equation}C = \frac{\text{ReLU}(\mathbf{F_{h} F^T_{h}})}{||\mathbf{F_{h}}||^2} - \mathbf{I}, \label{Eq-14}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{I}$ represents the identity matrix which is used to emphasise the irrelevance between each learned hyper-joint pair.}
elements.sent-2411-132: Specifically, in Divergence Loss, we measure the differences of hyper-joints with the mean of the cosine matrix $C$ in each layer.
elements.sent-2411-133: The calculation can be formulated as: \begin{equation}L_h(C) = \frac{\sum^{V_h}_{i=1}\sum^{V_h}_{j=1} c_{i,j}}{(V_h-1)^2}, \quad c_{i,j} \in C, L = L_{CE} + \frac{1}{L}\sum^L_{l=1} L_h(C_l),\end{equation} where $V_h$ represents the number of hyper-joints we introduced.

elements.sent-2411-134: Additionally, to preserve the original physical topology, we use the AHC-Module solely to learn the topology between feature $\mathbf{F_h}$ and feature $\mathbf{F_{p }$.}
elements.sent-2411-135: Hyper-GCN consists of an embedding layer, 9 spatial-temporal convolution layers, a global average pooling, and an FC layer, as shown in Figure~\ref{HyperGC (c).}
elements.sent-2411-136: The embedding layer is utilised to map individual joints into a high-dimensional feature space for subsequent adaptive hyper-graph convolution.
elements.sent-2411-137: To preserve the position awareness of the embedded features, we introduce the position embedding in the embedding layer inspired by InfoGCN~\cite{InfoGCN .}
elements.sent-2411-138: The 9 layers of Hyper-GCN are categorised into 3 stages.
elements.sent-2411-140: Each layer consists of Multi-Scale Hyper-graph convolution (MS-HGC) and Multi-Scale Temporal convolution (MS-TC)~\cite{CTR-GCN .}
elements.sent-2411-141: In stage 1, the number of channels is set as 128.
elements.sent-2411-142: Then it increases to 256 for stage 2 and stage 3.
elements.sent-2411-143: Dense connections are introduced to integrate deep and shallow features in each stage, which can smooth the obtained distribution against the hyper-vertices interaction at each layer.

elements.sent-2411-144: In this section, we conduct extensive experiments on standard benchmarking datasets to evaluate our Hyper-GCN, in the task of skeleton-based human action recognition.
elements.sent-2411-145: NTU-RGB+D 60 NTU-RGB+D 60~\cite{NTU60 is a large dataset widely used in skeleton-based human action recognition.}
elements.sent-2411-146: It consists of 56,880 different samples, categorised into 60 classes, obtained from the directed performances of 40 different actors.
elements.sent-2411-148: NTU-RGB+D 120 NTU-RGB+D 120~\cite{NTU120 is am extention of NTU 60, which introduce 57,367 new action samples.}
elements.sent-2411-149: It consists of 114,480 different samples, categorised into 120 classes instead of 60 classes.
elements.sent-2411-150: The number of actors increase to 106.
elements.sent-2411-152: The Northwestern-UCLA (NW-UCLA) dataset~\cite{NW-UCLA contains 1494 video clips, which is categorised into 10 classes.}
elements.sent-2411-153: It is obtained through 3 cameras with different camera views and is performed by 10 actors.
elements.sent-2411-154: As the proposed benchmark, for 3 camera views, 2 camera views are used for training, and the remaining 1 is used for validation.
elements.sent-2411-155: Our training and evaluation stages are on a single RTX 3090 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-156: In terms of the training phase, Hyper-GCN is optimised by Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) with Nesterov momentum set at 0.9 and a weight decay at 0.0004.
elements.sent-2411-157: Our implementation uses label smooth cross-entropy loss with the Divergence Loss we proposed.
elements.sent-2411-158: We set a total of 140 epochs with the start 5 warm-up epochs.
elements.sent-2411-159: The initial learning rate is 0.05, which is reduced to 0.005 at epoch 110 and to 0.0005 at epoch 120.
elements.sent-2411-160: The training batch size is set to 64 for NTU RGB+D and NTU RGB+D 120, and 16 for Northwestern-UCLA.
elements.sent-2411-161: Multi-stream ensembles proposed in ~\cite{Multi-Streams have been proven effective by most of the existing state-of-the-art methods.}
elements.sent-2411-162: We also use a 4-streams ensemble to evaluate our model.
elements.sent-2411-163: The detailed results are reported in Table \ref{coparison_sota .}
elements.sent-2411-164: To the best of our knowledge, a comparison with SOTA shows that our proposed Hyper-GCN achieves the SOTA level on each dataset.
elements.sent-2411-165: Especially, we outperform the involved competitors on NTU-RGB+D 120.
elements.sent-2411-166: This demonstrates the effectiveness and merits of Hyper-GCN.
elements.sent-2411-167: We conduct ablation experiments and report visualisations to analyse the effectiveness of each component of our design.
elements.sent-2411-168: All the ablation study is evaluated on the benchmark of NTU-RGB+D 120, using the joint modality.

elements.sent-2411-170: Multi-Scale Hyper-graph: As we mentioned, Multi-Scale Hyper-graph Convolution (MS-HGC) consists of an 8-branch parallel architecture.
elements.sent-2411-171: In particular, each branch corresponds to a hyper-graph at a different scale.
elements.sent-2411-172: The hyper-graphs at different scales represent the maximum number of hyper-edges each joint is contained in.
elements.sent-2411-173: They reflect the varying degrees of aggregation in the constructed topology.
elements.sent-2411-174: The different scales of hyper-graphs are determined by the hyper-parameter $K$ that we define.
elements.sent-2411-175: Therefore, to explore how to set the hyper-parameter $K$ for the branches in MS-HGC, we design the ablation experiments to investigate the impact of different scales on the model's recognition ability, as well as to compare the results by defining a single scale.
elements.sent-2411-176: The results are reported in Table~\ref{comparison_AHC .}
elements.sent-2411-177: When $K$ equals 1, each hyper-edge contains only one joint, which essentially eliminates any interaction between joints.
elements.sent-2411-178: Therefore, we do not set K to 1.
elements.sent-2411-179: Observing the design of the single scale hyper-graph, it becomes clear that increasing $K$ does not always yield better performance.
elements.sent-2411-180: A very large $K$ means that the maximum number of hyper-edges each joint is contained in increases, which in turn causes the number of joints in each hyper-edge to grow.
elements.sent-2411-181: Even though these "extra" joints may be optimised for minimal weight, they are likely to act as "noise", disrupting the information interaction represented by the hyper-edge.
elements.sent-2411-182: Furthermore, compared to a single-scale design, it is evident that the multi-scale design of MS-HGC outperforms the single scale design.
elements.sent-2411-183: For the complex and diverse characteristics of human action, multi-scale hyper-graphs in the spatial dimension can better capture features at multiple levels.
elements.sent-2411-184: Therefore, we visualise the Top-1 accuracy of selected human action categories as shown in Figure~\ref{class_visualization .}
elements.sent-2411-185: This is especially advantageous for recognising actions with varying degrees of motion.
elements.sent-2411-186: For instance, for actions like "Brushing teeth" or "Salutation", where only local joints are in motion, the smaller-scale hyper-graphs focus more on the local joints.
elements.sent-2411-187: In contrast, for actions like "Stand up" and "Jump up", where the entire body is engaged, a larger-scale hyper-graph can capture information between distant joints.
elements.sent-2411-188: This also inspired us to adopt the AHC-Module in different branches to complementarily learn hyper-graph topology at different scales.

elements.sent-2411-199: Architecture of Backbone: We validate the effectiveness of performing dense connections in the backbone.
elements.sent-2411-200: The experimental results are collected in Table~\ref{model .}
elements.sent-2411-201: The dense connections in the experiments are only added within each stage.
elements.sent-2411-202: It is observed that dense connections in shallow layers do not improve the performance as much as in deep layers.
elements.sent-2411-203: As the layers go deeper, the number of channels gradually increases and the variance of information extracted from each layer becomes steeper.
elements.sent-2411-204: This suggests that the deeper stages require dense connections to modulate the variance between features.
elements.sent-2411-205: For example, the performance introducing dense connections at stage 1 and stage 2 is close to adding the dense connection only at stage 3.
elements.sent-2411-206: To further validate that Hyper-GCN increases the efficiency of information interaction, we perform t-SNE projections on the output features of each layer, as shown in Figure~\ref{t-SNE .}
elements.sent-2411-207: Notice that the semantic information represented by the last layer features of Hyper-GCN is very convergent.
elements.sent-2411-208: This represents that the semantic information of each joint is adequately conveyed in Hyper-GC.
elements.sent-2411-209: The joints with similar semantic can prevent sacrificing the information after global average pooling.
elements.sent-2411-210: In this paper, we propose a Hyper-graph Convolutional Network for skeleton-based human action recognition.
elements.sent-2411-211: To exploit the implicit topology of multivariate synergy between joints, we introduce Multi-Scale Hyper-graph Convolution and virtual connections.
elements.sent-2411-212: We also introduce dense connections to fuse shallow and deep features.
elements.sent-2411-213: We carry out experiments on the dataset NTU-RGB+D 60 \& 120, NW-UCLA to validate the effectiveness of Hyper-GCN.
elements.sent-2411-214: The experimental analysis verifies that our design can improve the recognition performance.
elements.sent-2411-215: To the best of our knowledge, Hyper-GCN achieves the SOTA performance on 3 public datasets.
elements.sent-2411-216: The involvement of hyper-graph modelling indeed extends existing GCN-based action recognition paradigms.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
elements.sent-2411-217: However, most of the existing GCNs rely on the binary connection of two neighboring vertices (joints) formed by an edge (bone), overlooking the potential of constructing multi-vertex convolution structures.
elements.sent-2411-218: Although some studies have attempted to utilize hyper-graphs to represent the topology, they rely on a fixed construction strategy, which limits their adaptivity in uncovering the intricate latent relationships within the action.
elements.sent-2411-219: In this paper, we address this oversight and explore the merits of an adaptive hyper-graph convolutional network (Hyper-GCN) to achieve the aggregation of rich semantic information conveyed by skeleton vertices.
elements.sent-2411-220: In particular, our Hyper-GCN adaptively optimises the hyper-graphs during training, revealing the action-driven multi-vertex relations.
elements.sent-2411-221: The results of experiments conducted on the NTU-60, NTU-120, and NW-UCLA datasets demonstrate the merits of our Hyper-GCN, compared to the state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-222: Specifically, we outperform the existing solutions on NTU-120, achieving 90.5\% and 91.7\% in terms of the top-1 recognition accuracy on X-Sub and X-Set.
elements.sent-2411-223: Compared to RGB and optical flow images, skeleton data, which represents the basic physical structure of a human being, is of lower dimension, conveying human action with higher efficiency.

In (a), dotted lines represent the virtual connections. Each coloured part containing multiple joints represents a hyper-graph with hyper-edges. (b) provides an intuitive comparison between normal graph convolution and hyper-graph convolution operations with the same connectivity degree.} \label{motivation}
elements.sent-2411-224: Accordingly, the graph convolutional network (GCN) is typically introduced to aggregate feature information conveyed by skeleton joints~\cite{ST-GCN, HD-GCN, BlockGCN .}
elements.sent-2411-225: In terms of data representations, skeleton joints are also one-dimensional sequence data, similar to language.
elements.sent-2411-226: Therefore, with the rise of recent self-attention techniques~\cite{Transformer, VIT , several Transformer-based action recognition solutions \cite{ST-TR, TSTE, DSTA-Net, IIP-Transformer have been released.}}
elements.sent-2411-227: Transformer considers skeletal joints as tokens and uses attention maps to reflect the topology.
elements.sent-2411-228: These methods have achieved excellent performance but sacrifice much higher GFLOPS and parameters than GCN.
elements.sent-2411-229: Besides the physical skeleton topology, attempts have been made to explore the implicit relationships among joints~\cite{2s-AGCN, CTR-GCN, InfoGCN, HD-GCN , suggesting different variants of skeleton topology.}
elements.sent-2411-230: As shown in Figure~\ref{motivation (a), in a hyper-graph, a hyper-edge can link more than two vertices.}
elements.sent-2411-231: This linking has the capacity to represent complex collaborative relations among human joints.
elements.sent-2411-232: In contrast, in a hyper-graph convolution, the information of the green vertex spreads to all the vertices, creating an extended receptive field.
elements.sent-2411-233: Theoretically, by modelling a skeleton using a hyper-graph for the aggregation of joint information, improved efficiency can be obtained during learning the action semantics.
elements.sent-2411-234: However, the key to achieving enhanced information aggregation by hyper-graph convolution lies in the conformity of the hyper-graph structure to the real topology of human action.
elements.sent-2411-235: Several studies \cite{HypergraphNN, SHypergraphGCN attempt to adopt a hyper-graph to model the skeleton topology, mostly based on superficial prior knowledge and an unfounded definition of the structure of hyper-graph.}
elements.sent-2411-236: Distinguishing our approach from the existing hyper-graph-based methods, we design an adaptive solution for constructing the hyper-graph with virtual connections.
elements.sent-2411-237: It can construct a Non-uniform hyper-graph specific to each individual action type.

elements.sent-2411-249: For GCN-based approaches, ST-GCN~\cite{ST-GCN proposes to represent the topology with an adjacency matrix of 3 subsets by modeling the spatio-temporal relevance.}

elements.sent-2411-265: Constructing the hyper-graph incidence matrix is critical to understanding human actions.
elements.sent-2411-266: As shown in Figure~\ref{HyperGC (b), we design an Adaptive Non-uniform Hyper-graph (A-NHG) Construction.}
elements.sent-2411-267: Given the general feature $\mathbf{X}\in\mathbb{R ^{N \times C}$ of spatial skeleton joints, $N$ represent the number of joints, $C$ represent the channels.}
elements.sent-2411-268: Our A-NHG construct the hyper-edge with each joint as the center of mass, and a total of $N$ hyper-edges are constructed.
elements.sent-2411-269: A-NHG utilises the Euclidean distance to measure the joint difference.
elements.sent-2411-270: We use one mapping function $\Phi \in \mathbb{R ^{C \times C_h}$ to embed the features into the subspace for constructing the hyper-graph.}
elements.sent-2411-271: Based on $X_H \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times C_h}$, we define the set of joints $\mathcal{V_H = \{v_1 \in \mathbb{R ^{C_h , v_2 \in \mathbb{R ^{C_h , ...}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-272: Given $v_i, v_j \in \mathcal{V_H}$, each element $m_{i,j}$ in the distance matrix $M\in\mathbb{R ^{N \times N}$ can be obtained as follows: \begin{equation}m_{i,j} = m_{j,i} = ||v_i - v_j||^2_2.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-273: In principle, we need to transform the distance matrix into a probability incidence matrix, which determines whether the specific joints are contained with the same hyper-edges.
elements.sent-2411-274: For one joint, we retain only the $K$ nearest hyper-edges represented by joints in the semantic space for probability estimation.
elements.sent-2411-275: And $K$ is the hyper-parameter of A-NHG.
elements.sent-2411-276: Given the row vector $\mathbf{m _i$ in the distance matrix $M$, which represents the distances between joint $i$ and the other joints.}
elements.sent-2411-277: We select the indices of the minimum $K$ joints from $\mathbf{m _i$ and add them to the set $set_{i}$.}
elements.sent-2411-278: Based on $set_{i}$, $h_{i,j}$ in the incidence matrix $H \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times N}$, can be calculated as follows: \begin{equation}h_{i,j} =  \frac{exp(-m_{i,j})} {\sum_{k \in set_{i}}{exp(-m_{i,k})}}, j \in set_{i}, 0, j \notin set_{i} \end{equation} In contrast to uniform hyper-graph (every hyper-edge contains the same number of joints), our approach limits the maximum number of every joint that can be contained by hyper-edges.}
elements.sent-2411-279: Obviously, the number of joints contained in each hyper-edge constructed in this way is non-fixed, allowing the hyper-edges to capture more differentiated associations.
elements.sent-2411-280: The entire process for example with $K$ = 3 is illustrated in the Figure~\ref{A-NHG .}
elements.sent-2411-281: To accommodate the semantic information reflected by different group channels, we further propose the Multi-head Hyper-graph Convolution (M-HGC), as shown in Figure~\ref{HyperGC (a).}
elements.sent-2411-282: We use separate branches to independently perform hyper-graph convolution on the topologies represented by the multi-head hyper-graphs, thereby enhancing the computational efficiency of the Hyper-GCN.

elements.sent-2411-308: Hyper-graph Construction: The Non-uniform hyper-graphs with hyper-parameter $K$ represent the maximum number of hyper-edges contained in each joint.
elements.sent-2411-309: In general, it is common to adopt a uniform hyper-graph with hyper-parameter $K$.
elements.sent-2411-310: Therefore, to explore how to set the hyper-parameter $K$ for the adaptive hyper-graph in M-HGC, we design the ablation experiments to investigate the impact of hyper-parameter $K$ on the model's recognition ability, where both uniform and non-uniform hyper-graphs are considered.
elements.sent-2411-311: The results are reported in Table \ref{comparison_A-NHG .}
elements.sent-2411-312: In both uniform and non-uniform hyper-graphs, a very large $K$ means that more joints are contained in each hyper-edge.
elements.sent-2411-313: As the $K$ decreases, it is difficult for the hyper-edge to represent complex joint combination patterns.
elements.sent-2411-314: Therefore, setting $K = 5$ in uniform and $K = 9$ in Non-uniform achieves the best performance.
elements.sent-2411-315: The non-uniform hyper-graph of A-NHG outperforms the uniform hyper-graph, as the combination of skeleton joints in different actions is diverse.
elements.sent-2411-316: Furthermore, we visualise the hyper-graph inferred by Hyper-GCN on selected actions in Figure~\ref{hyper-graph-visualise .}
elements.sent-2411-317: The hyper-graph construction focuses on the motion joints that are most relevant to the corresponding action category.
elements.sent-2411-318: For “Kicking something” as part (d), the right leg joints and the left hand joint are modeled as a hyper-edge.
elements.sent-2411-319: Because in this action, the person's right hand previously extends, and the right leg swings relatively to the right hand.
elements.sent-2411-320: Similarly, in the action "Stand up" as shown in part (b), most joints of the upper body move relatively to right foot joint, which is not moving in the first half of frames.
elements.sent-2411-321: In addition, "Taking a selfie" and "Drinking water" are also consistent with the above view as Figure~\ref{hyper-graph-visualise (a), (c).}

elements.sent-2411-338: In this paper, we propose an Adaptive Hyper-graph Convolutional Network for skeleton-based human action recognition.
elements.sent-2411-339: To exploit the implicit topology of multivariate synergy between joints, we introduce the Adaptive Non-uniform Hyper-graph, the Multi-head Hyper-graph Convolution and virtual connections.
elements.sent-2411-340: The involvement of adaptive non-uniform hyper-graph modeling indeed extends existing GCN-based action recognition paradigms.. \nocite{* \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}}
elements.sent-2411-342: Skeleton-based human action recognition is a popular research topic in artificial intelligence, with practical applications in video understanding, video surveillance, human-computer interaction, robot vision, VR and AR~\cite{human_computer_interaction_1, human_computer_interaction_2, VR, skeletonViolenceDetection, wang2023visual, wang2023visual, skeletonVideoSurveillance, skeletonVideoSurvillance2 .}
elements.sent-2411-343: In general, a skeleton sequence contains a series of 2D or 3D coordinates, which can easily be collected by low-cost depth sensors or obtained by video-based pose estimation algorithms \cite{NTU60, NTU120, rajendran2024review, tang2023predicting .}
